name: La Mirada Circular Trail
name_original: Mirador de Orellán – running circular por el Monumento Natural de Las Médulas
description: Jog the difficult 6.0-mile La Mirada Circular Trail in Las Médulas, gaining 1081 feet with views from Mirador de Orellán.
region_intro: Jogging routes around Carucedo offer a unique experience through a landscape shaped by ancient Roman gold mining. The region is characterized by the distinct red clay mountains of Las Médulas, a UNESCO World Heritage site, and extensive oak and chestnut forests. Lago de Carucedo, a lake formed by these historical mining operations, provides calm waters and scenic pathways. The terrain varies from asphalted sections to dirt tracks, offering diverse running conditions. Best jogging routes around Carucedo The most popular jogging route is La Mirada Circular Trail, a 6.0 miles (9.6 km) trail that takes 1 hour 23 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers views of the unique red clay formations of Las Médulas. Another top favourite among local runners is Orellán Galleries loop from Las Médulas, a difficult 4.4 miles (7.1 km) path. This route explores remnants of the Roman mining efforts and passes through forested areas. Local runners also love the Las Médulas loop from Las Médulas, a 2.3 miles (3.6 km) trail leading through the historic mining landscape, often completed in about 24 minutes. Jogging around Carucedo is defined by the red clay mountains of Las Médulas, ancient chestnut and oak forests, and the tranquil Lago de Carucedo. The network offers options for easy runs around the lake as well as more challenging trails with elevation gain. The routes in Carucedo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from more than 4 reviews. More than 70 runners have used komoot to explore Carucedo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running routes around Carucedo?
A: The running routes in Carucedo offer a varied terrain. You'll find a mix of asphalted sections and dirt tracks, often winding through ancient chestnut and oak forests. The unique landscape of Las Médulas, with its reddish clay formations, also contributes to the distinct ground underfoot.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy jog?
A: Yes, Carucedo offers several easy running options. For a gentle and scenic run, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31885858" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Médulas loop from Las Médulas&lt;/a&gt;, which is 3.6 km long. Another excellent choice for an easy pace is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31882707" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago de Carucedo loop from Carucedo&lt;/a&gt;, covering approximately 5.2 km around the tranquil lake.

Q: What are the most challenging running trails in the Carucedo area?
A: For runners seeking a challenge, Carucedo has several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1052068583/la-mirada-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Mirada Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 9.6 km route with significant elevation gain, offering spectacular views of Las Médulas. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20148608" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orellán Galleries loop from Las Médulas&lt;/a&gt;, which is 7.1 km and explores remnants of the Roman mining operations.

Q: Can I find circular running routes around Carucedo?
A: Absolutely. Many of the running routes in Carucedo are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Popular circular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1052068583/la-mirada-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Mirada Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20148608" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orellán Galleries loop from Las Médulas&lt;/a&gt;, and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31882707" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago de Carucedo loop from Carucedo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What interesting landmarks or natural features can I see while running in Carucedo?
A: The running routes in Carucedo are rich with historical and natural sights. You'll jog through the unique red clay mountains of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4612988" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Médulas&lt;/a&gt;, a UNESCO World Heritage site, and past the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5550699" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago de Carucedo&lt;/a&gt;. Many trails also lead through ancient chestnut and oak forests, and some even pass by fascinating remnants of Roman mining like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3444033" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Encantada Cave and La Cuevona&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3444048" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orellán Galleries&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any viewpoints along the running trails in Carucedo?
A: Yes, the region offers breathtaking panoramic views. Many trails, especially those in Las Médulas, lead to elevated points. The Mirador de Orellán, for instance, provides stunning vistas of the ancient Roman gold mines and the surrounding landscape, making for a rewarding stop during your run.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Carucedo?
A: The running routes in Carucedo are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.8 stars. Runners often praise the unique historical landscape of Las Médulas, the serene beauty of Lago de Carucedo, and the variety of trails that cater to different fitness levels.

Q: Are the running routes in Carucedo suitable for families?
A: Yes, there are several family-friendly running options. The routes around &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5550699" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago de Carucedo&lt;/a&gt; are generally flat and easy, perfect for a leisurely jog with children. Shorter loops within Las Médulas, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31885858" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Médulas loop from Las Médulas&lt;/a&gt;, are also suitable for families looking for a less strenuous experience.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Carucedo?
A: The Carucedo region is generally welcoming to dogs on its trails, especially in natural areas like the chestnut and oak forests and around Lago de Carucedo. However, it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash and check for any specific local regulations or signage, particularly within the UNESCO World Heritage site of Las Médulas, to ensure a pleasant experience for everyone.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Carucedo?
A: The spring and autumn months are generally ideal for running in Carucedo. During these seasons, the weather is mild, and the natural scenery is particularly vibrant, with lush greenery in spring and rich autumnal colors in the chestnut and oak forests. Summer can be warm, so early mornings or late afternoons are recommended if running during that time.

Q: Are there options for longer distance running in the Carucedo area?
A: Yes, for those looking for longer runs, the extensive trail network in Las Médulas offers routes up to 14.5 kilometers, allowing for a more extensive exploration of the UNESCO site. You can also combine several shorter loops or explore routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38149844" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cúa River in Cacabelos loop from Carracedelo&lt;/a&gt;, which is over 10 km long.

Q: Is there public transport access to the running routes in Carucedo or Las Médulas?
A: While Carucedo is a small village, public transport options to the immediate trailheads might be limited. It's generally recommended to travel by car to access the various starting points for the running routes, especially those within Las Médulas. Parking is usually available near popular access points and visitor centers.
tips:

The route is circular, following the perimeter path to the Orellán viewpoint and then linking with the Valiñas path in the direction of La Cuevona and La Encantada.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging trail?
faqs.answer: The La Mirada Circular Trail is approximately 9.6 kilometers (6 miles) long. For jogging, it typically takes around 1 hour and 23 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and any stops you make.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the La Mirada Circular Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is rated as 'difficult' due to its length and significant elevation changes, with about 330 meters (1,083 feet) of ascent and descent. While specific terrain details aren't provided, expect varied surfaces suitable for jogging in a natural environment, likely including unpaved paths given the location within a natural monument.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers stunning views and passes by notable landmarks. You'll encounter the Pico de Placias, a summit offering panoramic vistas. A key highlight is the View of Las Médulas from the Mirador de Orellán, providing an iconic perspective of the ancient Roman gold mines.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails. Approximately 44% of the route follows the Camino Natural La Mirada Circular, 10% is part of the Camiño de Inverno, 4% covers the [CIMA LE17] Mirador de Orellán, and 3% runs along the Camino de Chao de Maixeiras.

faqs.question: Is the La Mirada Circular Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As the trail is located within the Monumento Natural de Las Médulas, it's generally advisable to check specific regulations regarding dogs. Typically, dogs are allowed on trails in natural parks but must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other visitors. Please verify current rules before your visit.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit Las Médulas for jogging?
faqs.answer: The best time to visit for jogging is generally during spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, the weather is milder, making for more comfortable conditions for a difficult trail. Summer can be very hot, and winter might bring colder temperatures and potentially less stable trail conditions.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Monumento Natural de Las Médulas?
faqs.answer: There is typically no entrance fee to access the Monumento Natural de Las Médulas itself, as it's an open natural area. However, certain specific attractions or facilities within the monument, such as the Mirador de Orellán cave or visitor centers, might have small fees or specific operating hours. It's always a good idea to check the official website for the latest information on any permits or fees required for specific activities or areas.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: While specific parking details for this exact Komoot tour are not provided, access to trails within the Monumento Natural de Las Médulas typically involves parking in designated areas near villages like Las Médulas or Orellán. Look for signs indicating public parking for visitors to the natural monument or the Mirador de Orellán.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner joggers?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating, a distance of nearly 10 km, and significant elevation gain of 330 meters, this trail is generally not recommended for beginner joggers. It requires a good level of fitness and experience with longer, more challenging routes.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a jog on this trail?
faqs.answer: For a difficult jogging trail of this length and elevation, you should bring appropriate trail running shoes, sufficient water (especially as water sources may be limited), energy snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for changing weather conditions. A map or GPS device is also recommended, as is a fully charged phone.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or considerations when jogging in Monumento Natural de Las Médulas?
faqs.answer: As a UNESCO World Heritage site and Natural Monument, visitors are expected to stay on marked trails, respect the natural and historical environment, and carry out all trash. Drones may be restricted, and open fires are strictly prohibited. Always check local signage and official park guidelines for the most current regulations.

waypoints.name: View of Las Médulas from the Mirador de Orellán
